message.content:
Record S6 in /home/polaris/wt/phase-s6 (branch chore/review-readiness-s6). Only commit if verify passed (collection_ok, only_build_artifacts_changed) and codex=S6-OK (S6-REVISE); else commit what's defensible + note the gap.
Stage the build/deps/docs/sbom files explicitly (git add of the specific new files + docs/; guard 0 tests/oracle staged; NO src/ runtime changes should be staged). Commit ("S6: reproducible build — hash-pinned deps + SBOM + build doc + two-build hash-match method") with standard trailers. Push -u origin; PR base gate-inversion (title "Code-review readiness: S6 reproducible build", body summarizing what's generated vs documented).
Return {commit_sha, pushed, pr_url, committed_or_blocked}.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The changed paths are exactly the build artifacts: README.md, .python-version, docs/review_readiness/reproducible_build.md, requirements.hashed.txt, sbom.cdx.json. No src/, tests/, or oracle changes. Let me read the build doc to understand the two-build hash-match method and verify the artifacts internally.

content.content:
1	# Reproducible build & supply-chain provenance
2	
3	Status: **S6 (review-readiness) — supply-chain hardening.**
4	Scope of this document: **build / dependency / documentation artifacts only.** No `src/`
5	runtime logic is involved in anything described here.
6	
7	This document is the single source of truth for how to build POLARIS from a pinned,
8	hash-verified dependency set, how to (re)generate the supply-chain artifacts, and how to
9	prove two independent builds produce byte-identical results.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## 1. Pinned Python version
14	
15	The runtime is pinned to a single interpreter patch version:
16	
17	| Pin | File | Value |
18	|---|---|---|
19	| Interpreter floor/exact | `.python-version` | `3.11.10` |
20	
21	`.python-version` is honoured by `pyenv` and by `uv` when it selects/provisions an
22	interpreter, so a fresh checkout resolves to the same interpreter used to compile the
23	lock and the SBOM.
24	
25	**Why `.python-version` and not `requires-python` in `pyproject.toml`:** `pyproject.toml`
26	in this repo is **tooling-only** (it carries `[tool.ruff]`, `[tool.mypy]`,
27	`[tool.interrogate]` and intentionally has **no `[project]` and no `[build-system]`
28	table**). The project is run from source; it is **not** a pip-installable/buildable
29	package. Adding a `[project]` table purely to hold `requires-python` would falsely signal
30	that the repo is a distributable wheel and would change tooling assumptions. The
31	interpreter contract is therefore expressed with `.python-version`, which matches how the
32	lock was originally compiled (`uv pip compile --python-version 3.11 ...`).
33	
34	---
35	
36	## 2. Dependency locking & cryptographic hashes
37	
38	### 2.1 Files
39	
40	| File | Pinned (`==`) | Cryptographic hashes | Role |
41	|---|---|---|---|
42	| `requirements.txt` | partial | no | Hand-maintained convenience set. **Not** for reproducible installs (self-described as non-minimal). |
43	| `requirements.lock` | yes (287 pkgs) | **no** | Original uv-compiled version lock. Kept for provenance/diffing. |
44	| `requirements.hashed.txt` | yes (287 pkgs) | **yes (5125 `--hash=sha256:` lines)** | **The reproducible-install source of truth.** |
45	
46	`requirements.hashed.txt` was **generated, not hand-written.** It was produced from the
47	existing pinned `requirements.lock` with `--generate-hashes`, so every one of the 287
48	pinned versions is carried through unchanged and each is now bound to its full set of
49	PyPI artifact SHA-256 hashes. Version drift between `requirements.lock` and
50	`requirements.hashed.txt` is **zero** (verified by diffing the `name==version` pin sets).
51	
52	### 2.2 Regenerating the hashed lock
53	
54	The lock is regenerated with [`uv`](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/). `uv` is the tool that
55	produced the original lock (see the header comment in `requirements.lock`).
56	
57	```bash
58	# From the repo root, with uv >= 0.11 available.
59	uv pip compile \
60	    --python-version 3.11 \
61	    --generate-hashes \
62	    requirements.lock \
63	    -o requirements.hashed.txt
64	```
65	
66	Compiling **from `requirements.lock`** (rather than re-resolving from the original
67	loose inputs `req-stripped.txt` + `requirements-v6.txt`, one of which no longer exists in
68	the tree) guarantees the pinned versions cannot move — the step only *adds hashes* to an
69	already-frozen version set.
70	
71	> Tooling note for this environment: `uv`, `pip-tools`, `cyclonedx-py`, and `pip-audit`
72	> are **not installed** in `/home/polaris/pipeline-env` (that env has pip 24.2 + Python
73	> 3.11.10). The artifacts committed here were generated by installing `uv` and
74	> `cyclonedx-bom` into a throwaway, isolated location (leaving `pipeline-env` untouched)
75	> and running the commands above/below. To reproduce on a clean machine:
76	>
77	> ```bash
78	> pip install 'uv>=0.11' 'cyclonedx-bom>=7'
79	> ```
80	>
81	> If `uv` is genuinely unavailable and only `pip-tools` is present, the equivalent is:
82	>
83	> ```bash
84	> pip-compile --generate-hashes --output-file requirements.hashed.txt requirements.lock
85	> ```
86	>
87	> Do **not** hand-edit hashes — they must always come from a resolver.
88	
89	### 2.3 Reproducible install (hash-enforced)
90	
91	```bash
92	# 1. Provision the pinned interpreter (pyenv/uv both honour .python-version).
93	python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate   # Python 3.11.10
94	
95	# 2. Install ONLY from the hashed lock, with hash verification MANDATORY.
96	pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
97	```
98	
99	`--require-hashes` makes pip **refuse** any package whose downloaded artifact does not
100	match a hash in the file, and refuse the whole file if any requirement lacks a hash. This
101	is the property that turns a version pin into a tamper-evident supply-chain control.
102	`--no-deps` is safe because the hashed lock is already a fully-resolved, transitively
103	complete set.
104	
105	---
106	
107	## 3. Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)
108	
109	| File | Format | Components | Reproducible |
110	|---|---|---|---|
111	| `sbom.cdx.json` | CycloneDX 1.5 (JSON) | 287 | yes — byte-identical across runs |
112	
113	`sbom.cdx.json` was **generated** from `requirements.hashed.txt` (the pinned+hashed
114	source of truth), not from the possibly-drifted live environment. Every component carries
115	a `pkg:pypi/...` PackageURL and its artifact hashes.
116	
117	### 3.1 Regenerating the SBOM
118	
119	```bash
120	# From the repo root, with cyclonedx-bom (cyclonedx-py) installed.
121	python -m cyclonedx_py requirements \
122	    --output-reproducible \
123	    --sv 1.5 \
124	    --of JSON \
125	    -o sbom.cdx.json \
126	    requirements.hashed.txt
127	```
128	
129	`--output-reproducible` strips the non-deterministic fields (random `serialNumber`,
130	wall-clock timestamps), which is what lets two runs be byte-identical — verified here:
131	regenerating `sbom.cdx.json` a second time produced a `diff`-clean, byte-identical file.
132	
133	An equivalent SBOM can be exported directly by `uv`:
134	
135	```bash
136	uv export --format cyclonedx1.5 --no-emit-project -o sbom.cdx.json
137	```
138	
139	(Prefer the `cyclonedx_py requirements` form above so the SBOM is bound to exactly the
140	committed `requirements.hashed.txt` rather than to a `uv.lock`, which this repo does not
141	carry.)
142	
143	### 3.2 Vulnerability audit (documented step)
144	
145	`pip-audit` is not installed in `pipeline-env`. To audit the pinned set against advisory
146	databases:
147	
148	```bash
149	pip install pip-audit
150	pip-audit -r requirements.hashed.txt            # audit the lock directly
151	# or, against the CycloneDX SBOM:
152	pip-audit --format cyclonedx-json --input sbom.cdx.json
153	```
154	
155	This is a **documented manual step**, not an artifact committed by S6.
156	
157	---
158	
159	## 4. Two-build hash-match verification
160	
161	The goal: prove the build is reproducible by building it **twice in independent clean
162	environments**, hashing the produced artifacts, and diffing. Any difference is a
163	non-determinism bug to be chased down.
164	
165	This mirrors the **deterministic-oracle byte-diff** precedent already in the repo
166	(`tests/oracle/demo_artifact_diff.py`), which freezes the LLM I/O with a cassette so the
167	only thing that can change an output artifact is the code, then asserts a **byte-identical**
168	replay on a no-op and a **diff** on a real change. Here we apply the same
169	"byte-compare final observable outputs" discipline to *build* artifacts instead of
170	pipeline outputs.
171	
172	### 4.1 Method
173	
174	```bash
175	# --- Build A: clean env #1 ---
176	git clean -xdf && python -m venv .venvA && source .venvA/bin/activate
177	pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
178	python -m cyclonedx_py requirements --output-reproducible --sv 1.5 --of JSON \
179	    -o /tmp/sbomA.cdx.json requirements.hashed.txt
180	sha256sum requirements.hashed.txt /tmp/sbomA.cdx.json > /tmp/hashesA.txt
181	deactivate
182	
183	# --- Build B: clean env #2 (fresh checkout / fresh venv) ---
184	git clean -xdf && python -m venv .venvB && source .venvB/bin/activate
185	pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
186	python -m cyclonedx_py requirements --output-reproducible --sv 1.5 --of JSON \
187	    -o /tmp/sbomB.cdx.json requirements.hashed.txt
188	sha256sum requirements.hashed.txt /tmp/sbomB.cdx.json > /tmp/hashesB.txt
189	deactivate
190	
191	# --- Compare: must be byte-identical ---
192	diff /tmp/sbomA.cdx.json /tmp/sbomB.cdx.json && echo "SBOM: byte-identical"
193	diff <(cut -d' ' -f1 /tmp/hashesA.txt) <(cut -d' ' -f1 /tmp/hashesB.txt) \
194	    && echo "HASH-MATCH: two builds agree"
195	```
196	
197	- The **dependency layer** is verified reproducible by `--require-hashes`: both builds
198	  install exactly the same artifacts (same SHA-256s) or fail.
199	- The **SBOM artifact** is verified reproducible by the `diff` on `sbomA` vs `sbomB`;
200	  `--output-reproducible` is what makes this hold. This was confirmed in-place for S6:
201	  regenerating the SBOM twice yielded a byte-identical file.
202	
203	### 4.2 Recorded S6 artifact digests
204	
205	Reference digests for the artifacts committed in S6 (recompute with `sha256sum` to
206	verify a checkout matches):
207	
208	```
209	requirements.hashed.txt : eff7c152c721eeeea043b568e7b92310bbdeb278db61c6af7f7f3b7fc7b69a69
210	sbom.cdx.json           : fdfbc55f454f04fa4b6f94fdcc595bd29c32b2cf9a5ca2d4aa10abd48c0a86ce
211	```
212	
213	> If you regenerate these files with a newer `uv`/`cyclonedx-bom` release, the digests
214	> above may legitimately change (tool version, hash-set completeness). The invariant that
215	> must always hold is **two builds on the same tool versions agree byte-for-byte**, and
216	> **the pinned `name==version` set never drifts from `requirements.lock`.**
217	
218	---
219	
220	## 5. What is automated vs a documented manual step (honesty ledger)
221	
222	| Item | S6 status |
223	|---|---|
224	| `.python-version` pin (`3.11.10`) | **Committed artifact.** |
225	| `requirements.hashed.txt` (287 pkgs, 5125 real SHA-256 hashes, zero version drift) | **Generated & committed** (real, from `uv`). |
226	| `sbom.cdx.json` (CycloneDX 1.5, 287 components, reproducible) | **Generated & committed** (real, from `cyclonedx_py`). |
227	| Hashed-lock regeneration command | **Documented** (§2.2) — re-runnable. |
228	| SBOM regeneration command | **Documented** (§3.1) — re-runnable, verified byte-identical. |
229	| `--require-hashes` install command | **Documented** (§2.3). |
230	| Two-build hash-match procedure | **Documented** (§4) — SBOM leg verified in-place. |
231	| `pip-audit` vulnerability scan | **Documented manual step** (§3.2) — tool not installed in `pipeline-env`. |
232	
233	`uv`, `cyclonedx-bom`, and `pip-audit` are **not** part of `pipeline-env`; the two
234	committed artifacts were produced by installing `uv` + `cyclonedx-bom` into an isolated
235	throwaway location so the runtime environment was never mutated. No hashes or SBOM
236	components were ever hand-written — everything committed came out of a resolver/generator.
237	
238	---
239	
240	## 6. Build-doc pointer fix
241	
242	The README "Quick start" installs the hand-maintained, un-hashed `requirements.txt`. For
243	a **reproducible, supply-chain-verified** install, use the hashed lock instead:
244	
245	```bash
246	python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate      # Python 3.11.10 (see .python-version)
247	pip install --require-hashes --no-deps -r requirements.hashed.txt
248	```
249	
250	`requirements.txt` remains as a developer convenience; `requirements.hashed.txt` is the
251	build-of-record.
252

content.text: There are 11 collection errors. I need to determine whether these are pre-existing (baseline) errors unrelated to S6, since S6 changed no src/ or tests/. Let me check the baseline test state doc and confirm these errors exist on the base commit too.

content.content:
# Baseline — test suite state (Phase 0-A, read-only)

**Commit:** d76a0ea (branch gate-inversion) · **Python:** 3.11.10 · **pytest:** 8.4.1

## Collection
- **16,501 tests collected** (corrects the audit's "1,082" — that was a file/subset count).
- **23 modules FAIL to collect.**

### Root cause of collection failures
`src/polaris_graph/audit_ir/registry.py:132` runs `_RUNS = _build_runs()` **at import time**, which
validates that historical run-output directories exist and raises `RegistryError` when they don't
(e.g. `outputs/full_scale_v30_phase2_run14/clinical/clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm`, absent in a clean
checkout). Any module importing `registry`/`live_server` then fails to import.
→ **Import-time filesystem validation that raises = non-portability anti-pattern.** Fix: make the
registry lazy (build on first use, not at import) and fail soft. (Work item, Phase 0/1.)
Plus 1 playwright "browser not installed" error (test-infra: needs `playwright install`).

## Execution — clean subset (`tests/unit`, `-m "not slow and not api and not live"`, continue-on-collection-errors)
| Run | passed | failed | errors | wall |
|-----|--------|--------|--------|------|
| 1   | 1343   | 104    | 103    | 226.8s |
| 2   | 1343   | 104    | 103    | 226.1s |

- **DETERMINISTIC** — identical across both runs → **not flaky**; failures are real, reproducible debt.
- Pass rate on this subset ≈ **86.6%** (1343 / 1550).
- `errors` (103) are mostly environment/import (playwright browser, registry chain) — setup-fixable.
- `failures` (104) are assertion mismatches — real test/code drift, needs triage.

## Honest implication for Telus
The suite is **NOT green out-of-the-box**. Presenting "16,501 tests" without saying they don't all
pass would fail the first `pytest`. The fix path is clear (deterministic, not flaky): (1) make
`registry` import lazy, (2) `playwright install` in CI, (3) triage the 104 assertion failures.
This is why the plan runs the baseline before claiming anything.
